headline: New Jersey Bans Grocery Stores From Using Shoppers' Personal Data to Set Prices
meta_description: New Jersey has enacted legislation preventing grocery stores from using shoppers' personal data to set individualized prices, protecting consumer fairness.
summary_short: New Jersey takes action to prevent price discrimination. Grocery stores can no longer use shoppers' personal information to charge different prices to different customer…
body:
New Jersey has banned grocery stores from using shoppers' personal data to set individualized prices. The legislation represents a significant step toward protecting consumers from discriminatory pricing practices that have become increasingly common as retailers collect more information about their customers.

The move addresses growing concerns about how personal data—gathered through loyalty programs, purchase history, and other tracking methods—is being weaponized to charge different prices to different shoppers. What one customer pays at checkout may differ from what another pays, based on algorithmic pricing tied to their shopping patterns and demographics.

This action by New Jersey signals a broader conversation about data ethics and fair pricing in retail. As more states consider consumer protection measures, the legislation offers a model for preventing corporations from exploiting personal information for profit at the expense of individual shoppers.
quick_facts: New Jersey banned personalized pricing by grocery stores
Legislation prevents using shoppers' personal data for prices
Addresses algorithmic price discrimination in retail
